period_focus: 2016 paid-entry controversy, 2025 price/social-license risk, 2026 global-festival model
evidence_level: B+ mixed success/price governance, C social-media-origin price incident until vendor contract proof
short_verdict: A successful/global festival can still carry price, booth and citizen social-license risk. Current public evidence supports a price-governance and resident-inconvenience dossier, not a proven subcontractor-failure claim.
why_it_matters: It shows the tradeoff between cost recovery through paid access and public social license.
It shows how a free-entry model can still use paid micro-experiences while managing price fairness.
It links big success headlines to field-level dissatisfaction risks: food price, price display, shuttle/traffic burden and crowd bottlenecks.
It gives a concrete booth-control outcome: a controversial food truck was expelled after price complaints.

stakeholders.commercial_nodes: food trucks, food booths, paid experience operators
stakeholders.contractor_causality_status: not proven from current public evidence

causal_chain.evidence: Yonhap reported the city used budget pressure and festival-quality improvement as reasons for full paid entry, with adult admission at 10,000 KRW, while citizen-use limits and screen/fence controversy produced opposition.
KTO lists the festival as free admission with some paid experience programs such as bridge crossing, wish lantern and floating lantern activities.
Media coverage of the MCST/KTO global-festival selection says the festival will receive 800M KRW per year for three years and target 2M visitors including 80,000 foreigners by 2028.

Evaluation coverage reports 1.72M visitors, 62.5% outside visitors, 143,383 KRW average spend, about 240B KRW regional economic effect and improved satisfaction, while noting 72.8% day/short visitors and need for stay-type content.

Yonhap reported Jinju expelled the food truck that sold a small 10,000 KRW chicken-gangjeong after field verification; it also reported the food-truck rent rumor was checked and rent was about 125,000 KRW per day.
Local reporting described repeated price/no-display complaints, resident frustration with shuttle/traffic guidance and crowded bottlenecks around weekend evening paths.

not_supported_claims: A subcontractor caused the price controversy.
Free entry eliminated all price-governance risk.
Global-festival selection proves visitor satisfaction without field-level controls.
High booth rent caused the 10,000 KRW chicken-gangjeong price.
next_evidence_needed: Food-truck/booth selection notice, fee schedule and operator contract
Civil complaint counts by price, traffic, crowding and resident access
Official evaluation report methodology for visitor count, spend and economic effect
Traffic/shuttle plan and resident impact mitigation data
